coredata.dc:title: Design of “Turn On” fluorometric nanoprobe based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots modified with β-cyclodextrin and vitamin B6 cofactor for selective sensing of dopamine in human serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Alqarni, Ali O.
Alkahtani, Saad A.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel and rapid fluorometric nanoprobe was constructed for quantitation of dopamine (DA) in presence of biologically interfering compounds. The nanoprobe based on synthesis of yellow emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N@GQDs) by advanced thermal driven oxidation. After that, the synthesized N@GQDs was capped with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), followed by interaction with pyridoxal (PYL) vitamin B6 cofactor. This interaction resulted in diminishing the yellow fluorescence of β-CD/N@GQDs, and appearance of blue emission peak at 420 nm. Upon addition of DA, the blue emission of β-CD/N@GQDs was increased after excitation at λ = 330 nm. Under optimum conditions, the nanoprobe exhibited a linear range of 0.36–400 nM with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.117 nM. In addition, the fluorescent nanoprobe shows high selectivity and can be used for detection of DA in complicated biological matrices and human serum. This strategy might provide a potential tool for clinical diagnosis and biomedical research for DA related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
β-cyclodextrin
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Selectivity
Human serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N@GQDs) via thermal driven oxidation
Selectivity and application of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs nanoprobe
“Turn On” detection of DA by PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs nanoprobe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the ministry of education and the Deanship of Scientific Research , Najran University-Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for their financial and technical support under code number [ NU/MID/17/078 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The UV/Vis, excitation and emission spectra of β-CD/N@GQDs, (B) FTIR spectra of (a) N@GQDs, (b) β-CD and (c) β-CD/N@GQDs, (C) TEM image of β-CD/N@GQDs (inset is size distribution histogram) and (D) CVs of (a) 0.5 M KCl, (b) N@GQDs and (c) β-CD/N@GQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of β-CD/N@GQDs, DA/β-CD/N@GQDs, PYL, DA/PYL, DA, PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs and DA-PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra after addition of different concentrations of DA: (0) 0, (1) 5, (2) 50, (3) 100, (4) 150, (5) 200, (6) 250, (7) 300, (8) 350 and (9) 400 nM. (B) Calibration plot measured under conditions of 150 μL of β-CD capped N@GQDs, 200 μL of 5 mM PYL and total volume of 3 mL.
The selectivity of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs toward 350 nM DA and other interfering species (100 µM).
Representative diagram showing sensing of DA via PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs with the corresponding color changes.
Regression data of the calibration plots for quantitative determination of DA in standard solution and human serum using PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs probe (n = 5).
Comparison to existing methods for analysis of DA.

head.ce:title: Design of “Turn On” fluorometric nanoprobe based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots modified with β-cyclodextrin and vitamin B6 cofactor for selective sensing of dopamine in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudia Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudia Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel and rapid fluorometric nanoprobe was constructed for quantitation of dopamine (DA) in presence of biologically interfering compounds. The nanoprobe based on synthesis of yellow emissive nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N@GQDs) by advanced thermal driven oxidation. After that, the synthesized N@GQDs was capped with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), followed by interaction with pyridoxal (PYL) vitamin B6 cofactor. This interaction resulted in diminishing the yellow fluorescence of β-CD/N@GQDs, and appearance of blue emission peak at 420 nm. Upon addition of DA, the blue emission of β-CD/N@GQDs was increased after excitation at λ = 330 nm. Under optimum conditions, the nanoprobe exhibited a linear range of 0.36–400 nM with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.117 nM. In addition, the fluorescent nanoprobe shows high selectivity and can be used for detection of DA in complicated biological matrices and human serum. This strategy might provide a potential tool for clinical diagnosis and biomedical research for DA related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a vital neurotransmitter belonging to the class of catecholamine containing drugs [1]. The vitality of the drug is due to its importance as a neural conductor, and is considered one of biological indexes of some diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s, schizophrenia, and cardiovascular diseases [2]. Due to extremely similar structures and properties, the determination of DA in biological fluids suffers from several interferences caused by other interfering substances such as uric acid, ascorbic acid and some amino acids present in biological fluids [3]. Many reports estimated DA in different matrices such as chromatography [4,5], electrochemistry [6-9], and optical sensing [1,10–13]. Chromatographic methods have several disadvantages such as the need of highly skilled persons, solvent consumption, expensive instrumentation and exhaustive extraction steps. Despite the prominent sensitivity of electrochemical methods, the low stability of nanomaterials and poor reproducibility are the main drawbacks that restricted their clinical applications [14,15]. Optical sensing methods have several advantages such as good reproducibility, fast response, simplicity and ease of visualization. Many optical chemosensors were developed for analysis of DA such as carbon quantum dots [16–19], fluorescent organic nanoparticles [20], gold nanoclusters [21], and upconversion nanoparticles [22]. A number of colorimetric assays for analysis of HIS have been developed, but currently available colorimetric assays are inferior to fluorometric methods in terms of sensitivity and interferences. Several types of fluorescence probes based on carbon dots (CDs) have been developed for determining DA [16–19]. However, most of these probes are fluorescence turn-off probes. Fluorescence “Turn On” nanoprobes are less likely than fluorescence quenching sensors to give false positive signals and are therefore generally preferred [23].

Recently, fluorometric methods using carbon dots (CDs), metal based quantum dots (M-QDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively introduced for analysis of various compounds such as drugs, colorants, metals and mycotoxins [24–29]. GQDs are ideal zero dimensional carbon containing nanomaterials with superior electrical conductivity, optical properties and good biocompatibility [30,31]. They are favorable nanomaterials to replace traditional semiconductor such as carbon dots, quantum dots and organic dyes [32,33]. GQDs have been used in bioimaging, biosensing, and metal ion sensing because of their low toxicity, biocompatibility, water solubility, and possible grafting with various groups [34,35]. GQDs were doped with various heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur and others to enhance their optical properties in bioimaging and sensing [27,34–38].

Herein, a simple, rapid, highly selective and ultra-sensitive host–guest based fluorometric nanoprobe was developed for the analysis of DA. The nanoprobe based on modification of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N@GQDs) with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) and pyridoxal (PYL) as vitamin B6 cofactor (PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs). The β-CD/N@GQDs nanocomposite possesses strong yellow fluorescence and when interacted with PYL, a new peak appeared at 420 nm with blue fluorescence. It is reported that the conjugation between vitamin B6 precursors such as pyridoxal, pyridoxamine and pyridoxine diminishes the fluorescence of bovine serum albumin capped CdS QDs with shifting of fluorescence to shorter wavelengths [39].

Dopamine HCl, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glutathione, glycine, methionine, lysine, graphite powder and pyridoxal were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Rhodamine B, ethylenediamine, p-toluene sulfonyl chloride and β-cyclodetrin (β-CD) were purchased from Merck. Ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from El-Nasser, Egypt. All other common solvents and salts were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water with the resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments.

Spectrofluorophotometer RF-5301 PC (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) was used with the excitation and emission slit control set at slit width 5 nm that equipped with 1 cm quartz cell. FTIR spectra were recorded on Nicolet 6700 FT-IR Advanced Gold Spectrometer with KBr disc and DTGS detector. The morphology of N@GQDs was carried out by transmission electron microscope JEOL JEM-100CX II unit tungsten EM filament 120 kV assisted by high-quality imaging camera conducted with Digital Micrograph software system of Gatan Erlangshen ES500W (USA). Elemental analysis was carried out using OXFORD INA Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDX). Nicolet 6700 FTIR Advanced Gold Spectrometer, supported with OMNIC 8 software (Thermo Electron Scientific instruments Corp., Madison, WI USA) was used to record FTIR spectra. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was performed using a PW1729 Philips diffractometer interfaced with a computer control unit model PW1710 using copper source. The change in chemical species of N, S@GQDs was recorded by an ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) from Physical Electronics using Al Kα radiation photon energy at 1486.6 ± 0.2 Ev.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N@GQDs) via thermal driven oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (1 g) was dispersed in 100 mL double distilled water and sonicated for about 30 min to ensure complete dispersity. To this solution, FeCl3·6H2O (15 mg) and 30 mL of H2O2 (15.0 M) were add, and the mixture was heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The obtained solution was filtered through 0.22 μm filter paper, followed by freeze-drying. To prepare photoluminscent N@GQDs, 10 mL of the solution was mixed with 15 mL ethylenediamine and 25 mL water. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 8 h before filtration with 0.22 μm filter paper, and freeze-drying.

Alkaline solution of β-CD (100 mL, 0.05 M) was mixed with 20 mL of 0.02 M ethanolic solution of p-toluenesulfonyl chloride (p-tolyl SO2-Cl), and the mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature. To ensure complete precipitation and improve the yield, the filtrate was acidified with 1 M hydrochloric acid, and it was left overnight at room temperature [40].

To prepare β-CD capped N@GQDs, 1 mL of 0.2 mg mL−1 of N@GQDs and 5 mL of p-tolyl SO2-Cl/β-CD were mixed, and the pH of solution was adjusted to 12 with 1.0 M NaOH prior to filtration and freeze-drying. The quantum yield of yellow fluorescent β-CD/N@GQDs was calculated and was found to be 38%, compared to a reference material Rhodamine B.

Stock solution of β-CD/N@GQDs was prepared via dispersing 200 mg of the powder in 50 mL double distilled water. Then, 150 μL of the prepared β-CD/N@GQDs (4 mg mL−1) was mixed with 200 μL of 5 mM pyridoxal (vitamin B6 cofactor, PYL) and different aliquots (in μL) of DA, and the volume was completed to 3 mL with double distilled water at 25 °C. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at λem. = 420 nm after excitation at λ = 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and application of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs fluorometric nanoprobe was tested by addition of various possible interfering species to the fluorescent nanoprobe instead of DA. The interfering compounds that were tested are 100 μM uric acid, glycine, ascorbic acid, methionine, lysine, hydrogen peroxide and glutathione.

In order to evaluate the applicability of the proposed fluorometric nanoprobe, it was used to determine DA in human serum by spiking different amounts of DA, followed by measurement with the nanoprobe. All experimental protocols were reviewed and approved by the ethics of Assiut University clinics, Assiut, Egypt.

The morphology of N@GQDs was characterized using TEM. The TEM image (Fig. S1a) shows that the N-GQDs are homogeneously distributed. The particle size of N@GQD illustrated in Fig. S1b is in the range of 8–11.5 nm and the average lateral size, determined by the histogram, is 10.0 ± 0.5 nm (n = 70). The morphology of N@GQD is also examined PXRD where Fig. S1c exhibits a broad peak centered at 2θ = 23.7°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphene. Fig. S1d shows the FTIR spectra of N@GQDs. Peaks at 3380, 2840, 1725,1380 and 1080 cm−1 correspond to δ (OH), δ (CH2), δ (CO), δ (CC) and δ (CO), respectively. In addition, XPS survey of N@GQDs was introduced in Fig. S1e shows the XPS full scan survey of all materials exhibits C 1s, O 1s and N 1s peaks at 284 and 532 eV and 403 eV, respectively. EDX of N@GQDs was shown in Fig. S1f with identifiable peaks of C, O and N.

The β-CD/N@GQDs was characterized by UV–Vis, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR and cyclic voltammetry (CV). Broad band in UV–Vis spectrum with a maxima of 315 nm, corresponding to n-π* transition of CO (Fig. 1 A). The excitation of β-CD/N@GQDs at 465 nm showed an emission peak at 545 nm (Fig. 1A). The FTIR spectra of N@GQDs was recorded in Fig. 1B(a) where the bands at 3380, 2855–2925, 1670, 1420, and 1050 cm−1 correspond to υ (OH), υ (CH2), δ (OH), υ (CC) and υ (CN), respectively. From Fig. 1B(b), it is obvious that the FTIR spectrum of β-CD was identical to N@GQDs while the bands in a combined mixture have moved to 1650 and 3385 cm−1, which indicate the successful capping of N@GQDs with β-CD. The size of the synthesized β-CD/N@GQDs was characterized with TEM as shown in Fig. 1C. The prepared β-CD/N@GQDs were nearly uniform with size ~10 nm with narrow size distribution. To demonstrate the electrochemical behaviors of N@GQDs and β-CD/N@GQDs, the CVs of the β-CD and β-CD/N@GQDs were compared (Fig. 1D), and it was found that there is no clear redox peaks in the scanning potential range of −0.7 to 0.7 V. Fig. 1Da shows the CV of supporting electrolyte (0.5 M KCl) with no obvious redox peak at potential range of −0.7 to 0.7 V. After addition of N@GQDs to the supporting electrolyte, no significant change in the stripping current with the absence of observable peaks (Fig. 1Db) in the same potential range. After functionalization of N@GQDs with β-CD, no change in the stripping current was occurred that prove no electrochemical differences between N@GQDs and β-CD/N@GQDs (Fig. 1Dc). In addition, these results prove the stability of N@GQDs even after capping with β-CD/N@GQDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Turn On” detection of DA by PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The target analyte (DA) was determined via β-CD capped N@GQDs decorated with vitamin B6 cofactor pyridoxal (PYL). As shown in Scheme 1 , the β-CD linked to the surface of N@GQDs encapsulated PYL through formation of host–guest inclusion complexes [34,35]. The process of interaction between β-CD/N@GQDs and PYL resulted in diminishing the yellow color of β-CD/N@GQDs, which was “turned on” to blue emission upon addition of DA. The cofactor PYL can bind β-CD via multiple hydrogen bonds while its CHO group can recognize DA via formation of imine bond (Schiff’s base formation), which increase delocalization of electrons and provide detectable fluorescence signal for DA sensing [41,42].

The host–guest interaction between β-CD/N@GQDs and PYL was examined via recording the fluorescence spectra of β-CD/N@GQDs via mixing with 150 μL of the β-CD/N@GQDs and 200 μL of 5 mM PYL followed by dilution to 3 mL DDW. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence emission of β-CD/N@GQDs locates at emission wavelength of 550 nm and it is not affected by addition of DA. It was also seen that PYL showed emission peak at λ = 467 nm and its emission was slightly improved by addition of non-fluorescent DA. It was found that the fluorescence of β-CD/N@GQDs was decreased upon addition of PYL with the formation of a new peak at 420 nm [39]. Upon addition of DA to PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs system, the fluorescence intensity of the peak at 420 nm was largely increased, which may be attributed to the formation of highly conjugated system between β-CD/N@GQDs, PYL and DA.

Variables affecting the fluorescence sensing of DA using PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs were optimized including β-CD/N@GQDs and PYL concentrations and temperature. It is shown in Fig. S2 that the conditions affecting the sensing of DA are 4 mg mL−1, 5 mM and 25 °C as optimum values for β-CD/N@GQDs, PYL and temperature, respectively.

Linearity was performed by carrying out fluorescence titration experiments where different aliquots of DA were added to the solution of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs probes, prepared by mixing 150 μL of 4 mg mL−1 β-CD capped N@GQDs, 200 μL of 5 mM PYL and different aliquots of DA (in μL) followed by dilution to 3 mL double distilled water. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence emission of the probe was increased after DA addition from 0.357 to 400 nM with LOD of 0.117 nM. Moreover, the proposed sensor was used to determine the linear range, LOQ and LOD of DA in human serum after dilution with double distilled water as seen in Table 1 .

Intra-day and inter-day precision were evaluated in terms of RSD % as recommended by International Conference on Harmonization guidelines. The results shown in Table S1 confirm the precision of the method, as indicated by low values of RSD %.

The accuracy of the proposed fluorometric method was measured by comparison the results obtained with the reported method [12]. The statistical analysis of the results obtained by the reported and proposed methods shows no significant difference between them, confirming the accuracy of the proposed method (Table S2).

Robustness of the proposed fluorometric method was evaluated by slight variation in procedure parameters such as concentration of β-CD/N@GQDs and PYL. The results shown in Table S3 indicate that slight variations in concentration of β-CD/N@GQDs and PYL not affect the performance of the proposed fluorometric nanoprobe as indicated by recoveries % and RSD %.

In order to demonstrate the selectivity of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs toward DA detection in biological fluids, common interfering species with the physiological levels (10 μM) are considered, such as uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), lysine (LYS), glutathione (GLU), glycine (GLY), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and methionine (METH). As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs can be enhanced only by the addition of DA, and the other interfering species did not significantly affect the fluorescence emission of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs, therefore, exhibit a negligible effect on the determination of DA. This specific “Turn ON” fluorescence between DA and PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs might be due to the imine linkage formed between CHO of PYL and NH2 of DA, in addition the suitability of β-CD internal cavity for DA structure. The high selectivity of the fluorescence nanoprobe may be due to several reasons: (1) the presence of conjugated π electrons in DA increases the π-π conjugation with the synthesized nanoprobe leading to high fluorescent intensity [43], (2) the restriction in the structural flexibility within β-CD internal cavity triggered the fluorescence improvement [44] and (3) the absence of conjugation in different interfering amino acids e.g. lysine (LYS), glycine (GLY) and methionine (METH) do not favor the fluorescence intensity of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs nanoprobe.

To prove the ability of the fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of DA in biological matrices, the responses of PYL/β-CD/N@GQDs toward DA in 10-fold diluted serum samples were measured. It is obvious from Fig. S3 that the increase of the nanoprobe fluorescence followed the increase of DA concentration, suggesting that the nanoprobe can detect DA in complicated matrices with high selectivity. Moreover, the recoveries % and RSD % were listed in Table S4 confirm the accuracy and precision of the proposed method to determine DA in biological fluids.

In order to raise the importance of the proposed fluorometric nanoprobe, several parameters were compared with other reported methods for analysis of DA including linearity range and LOD. From Table 2 , it was found that the proposed fluorometric method has superiority over the tabulated reported methods for determination of DA.

Herein, a novel and rapid fluorometric method was developed for ultrasensitive and highly selective analysis of dopamine (DA). The fluorometric nanoprobe based on interaction between newly synthesized N@GQDs, β-CD and vitamin B6 cofactor pyridoxal (PYL) as a selective receptor for attachment of DA. The β-CD/N@GQDs shows an emission peak at 545 nm (yellow emission) when excited at wavelength of 465 nm. Upon attachment of PYL, the fluorescence emission was blue shifted with the development of a new emission peak at 420 nm (blue emission). Interestingly, addition of DA resulted in enhancement of blue emission at 420 nm, which was increased upon addition different concentrations of DA. Under the optimized conditions, the nanoprobe exhibited a linear range of 0.36–400 nM with LOD of 0.117 nM. The method was extended for analysis of DA in presence of interfering species and human serum. As a conclusion, the proposed nanoprobe exhibits simplicity, rapidity, sensitivity and selectivity, which are good performance parameters for analysis of DA with low cost.

Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the ministry of education and the Deanship of Scientific Research, Najran University-Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for their financial and technical support under code number [NU/MID/17/078].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119180.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Science

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Anal. Chem.

Small

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoclusters-poly (9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl) dots@zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) nanohybrid based probe for ratiometric analysis of dopamine
Anal. Chim. Acta
